items.q: In an exothermic reaction, what happens energetically with the bonds?
items.correct: Energy released forming new bonds exceeds energy absorbed breaking existing bonds.
items.misconception: Bond breaking releases energy and bond making absorbs energy.
items.random_wrong: The reactants get colder because they lose mass as energy.
items.why: Students confuse the direction: they think breaking is 'releasing' (like cracking something open releases contents), when actually breaking always requires energy input.

items.q: Can a single standard electrode potential be measured in isolation?
items.correct: No — electrode potentials are RELATIVE values measured against a reference (the standard hydrogen electrode, defined as 0 V).
items.misconception: Each electrode has an absolute potential that can be measured directly with a voltmeter.
items.random_wrong: Standard electrode potentials are measured by submerging the electrode in pure water.
items.why: Students think a voltmeter can give a single electrode's value; they don't grasp that potential difference requires two electrodes by definition.

items.q: In 1H NMR, what does the n+1 rule predict?
items.correct: A proton with n equivalent neighbouring protons will appear as a peak split into n+1 sub-peaks.
items.misconception: Splitting tells you how many protons are in the same chemical environment as the one giving the peak.
items.random_wrong: The number of peaks equals the total number of protons in the molecule.
items.why: Students confuse splitting (about NEIGHBOURING protons) with the number of equivalent protons producing the peak (which affects peak area/integration, not splitting).

items.q: What separation method works best for different coloured dyes in ink?
items.why: It's Chromatography. “Filtration” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which separation method would you use to remove iron from a mix of iron and sulfur?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Magnetism. “Distillation” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: On a chromatogram, how many spots does a pure substance produce?
items.why: Exact answer: 1. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
